coredata.dc:title: Novel functional fiber loaded with carbon dots for the deep removal of Cr(VI) by adsorption and photocatalytic reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Junfeng
Cheng, Yuanpeng
Guo, Caixia
Liu, Xiangting
Zhang, Bing
Yuan, Siguo
Huang, Jiajia
coredata.dc:description: A novel functional fiber (PAN-CDs) loaded with carbon dots (CDs) with excellent photoreduction and adsorption properties for Cr(VI) was prepared via an amidization reaction between the CDs' carboxyl groups and amine groups on polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based ion exchange fibers, which could completely preserve the fluorescence properties of the CDs. The photoluminescence (PL), photocatalysis and adsorption properties of PAN-CDs were characterized and analyzed. The PAN-CDs possess high adsorption capacity (297.6 mg/g) and excellent kinetic behavior (attaining adsorption equilibrium in 30 min) for Cr(VI) adsorption. Furthermore, the residual Cr(VI) (approximately 3 mg/L) after adsorption could be removed completely by subsequent photoreduction by the PAN-CDs. The Cr-saturated PAN-CDs could be easily separated by filtering and regenerated, with no observable decay of removal efficiency after five regeneration cycles. In addition, due to the PL quenching action of Cr(VI), the PAN-CDs can also be used as sensor for quantitative detection of trace Cr(VI) in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Cr(VI)
Adsorption
Photoreduction
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PAN-based ion exchange fiber (PAN-TETA)
Chemical grafting of CDs on PAN-TETA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51503184 and 51873198 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs; (b) C1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs, PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs; (c) thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of CDs, PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs under N2 and (d) water contact angle images of PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs in aqueous solution and PAN-CDs in solid state, (b) photographs of CDs in solution (0.10 mg/L), CDs in solid state, PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs taken under 365 nm irradiation, and PL spectra of (c) CDs in aqueous solution and (d) PAN-CDs excited at various wavelengths. Ex: excitation peak; Em: emission peak.

(a) Adsorption kinetics at the initial Cr(VI) concentration 100 mg/L and (b) adsorption isotherms at the initial Cr(VI) concentration from 20 to 500 mg/L of Cr(VI) on PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%). Adsorption conditions: volume 100 mL, temperature 30°C, fiber dosage 1.0 g/L and pH 2. t: adsorption time; C e: the final concentration of Cr(VI) in solution; Q e: the adsorption capacity.

(a) Adsorption and reduction of Cr(VI) by PAN-TETA, PAN-TETA+CDs and PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%), (b) details of the reduction of Cr(VI) in Fig. 4a, (c) effect of CDs loading weight on the reduction of Cr(VI) and (d) cyclic tests of Cr(VI) adsorption and reduction by PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%). Adsorption conditions: initial [Cr(VI)] 100 mg/L, volume 100 mL, temperature 30°C, fiber dosage 1.0 g/L and pH 2. C, C 1 and C 0: the concentration of Cr(VI) at the illumination time t, t = 30 min and the initial concentration of Cr(VI), respectively. [Cr(VI)]: the concentration of Cr(VI).
Schematic illustration of the proposed photocatalytic reduction mechanism of PAN-CDs. CB: conduction band; VB: valence band; e−: electron; h+: electron hole; Vis: visible light.
Illustration of the preparation process of the PAN-CDs. PAN: polyacrylonitrile; CDs: carbon dots; TETA: triethylenetetramine.
Comparison of Cr(VI) adsorption capacities with other adsorbents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPMC: hydroxypropyl methylcellulose; MC-N: magnetic carbon nanoadsorbents; ZVI: zero valent iron; CTNC: copolymer-templated nitrogen-doped carbons.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order parameters for the adsorption of Cr(VI) on PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Q e(exp): the experimental equilibrium adsorption capacity toward Cr(VI); k 1: the rate constant of pseudo-first-order adsorption; Q e(cal): the theoretically calculated equilibrium adsorption capacity toward Cr(VI); t 0.5: adsorption time to absorb half concentration of Cr(VI); k 2: the rate constant of pseudo-second-order adsorption; R 1 2 and R 2 2: the correlation coefficients of the corresponding fitted curves, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of isotherm parameters for the adsorption of Cr(VI) on PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Q max: the maximum adsorption capacity toward Cr(VI); b: Langmuir constant; K and n: Freundlich constants related to the energy of sorption and reaction bond energy, respectively.

head.ce:title: Novel functional fiber loaded with carbon dots for the deep removal of Cr(VI) by adsorption and photocatalytic reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel functional fiber (PAN-CDs) loaded with carbon dots (CDs) with excellent photoreduction and adsorption properties for Cr(VI) was prepared via an amidization reaction between the CDs' carboxyl groups and amine groups on polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based ion exchange fibers, which could completely preserve the fluorescence properties of the CDs. The photoluminescence (PL), photocatalysis and adsorption properties of PAN-CDs were characterized and analyzed. The PAN-CDs possess high adsorption capacity (297.6 mg/g) and excellent kinetic behavior (attaining adsorption equilibrium in 30 min) for Cr(VI) adsorption. Furthermore, the residual Cr(VI) (approximately 3 mg/L) after adsorption could be removed completely by subsequent photoreduction by the PAN-CDs. The Cr-saturated PAN-CDs could be easily separated by filtering and regenerated, with no observable decay of removal efficiency after five regeneration cycles. In addition, due to the PL quenching action of Cr(VI), the PAN-CDs can also be used as sensor for quantitative detection of trace Cr(VI) in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been demonstrated to be promising photocatalysts or modifiers for photocatalysis owing to their outstanding electronic properties since the seminal work of Li’s group in 2010 (Yu et al., 2016; Li et al., 2010). Compared with conventional semi-conductor photocatalysts (Ma et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018), CDs are characterized by low cytotoxicity, robust chemical inertness, low photobleaching, good cost effectiveness, excellent photostability, and two-photon induced fluorescence (Zhuo et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Cao et al., 2011). As a result, they have broad application prospects in photocatalytic degradation (Wang et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013), photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (Yu et al., 2014; Yeh et al., 2014), photocatalytic conversion of CO2 (Cao et al., 2011; Li et al., 2015), and organic photocatalysis (Li et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2015).

However, the separation and reuse of CDs are rather difficult due to their nanometer size and compatibility with solvents, which limit their large-scale industrial application. Loading CDs onto appropriate carriers may be a viable approach to overcome the difficult problem above. Unfortunately, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by most carriers (Qu et al., 2012), which leads to a dramatic decrease in the photocatalytic activity of CDs. Polymers, including commercially available paper, cotton, nylon fibers, and polyvinyl acetate, have been demonstrated to be suitable carriers for CDs, which could alleviate the fluorescence quenching effect of carriers on CDs (Qu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2014). In addition, the interaction between CDs and carriers has a great effect on the stability of CDs, and the CDs loaded on carriers through physical processes were found to be prone to loss, causing a decrease in the catalytic performance.

As one of the common heavy metal ions, Cr(VI) is widely used in electroplating, leather tanning, metal finishing, textile manufacturing, steel fabrication, paints, and pigments, etc. (Dai et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010). It is highly toxic to organisms and can cause cancer, nasal septum perforation, and kidney damage in human beings even at low concentrations. The Cr(VI) concentration in drinking water has been recommended to be below 0.05 mg/L by the World Health Organization (Dinker and Kulkarni, 2015). Among various technologies for the removal of Cr(VI), the ion exchange method is recognized as an efficient and simple approach for the resource recovery of Cr(VI) from water because of the low sludge volume produced and excellent adsorptive selectivity (Shi et al., 2009; Jachuła and Hubicki, 2013). However, it is difficult to satisfy the increasingly strict discharge specifications by use of only one technology (Shi et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2013). Photocatalytic reduction is another valid method to remove Cr(VI) from water. The Cr(VI) can be efficiently reduced to less toxic Cr(III), which can be easily removed from the aqueous phase via Cr(OH)3 precipitation under neutral or alkaline conditions (Djellabi et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2011). CDs have been shown to be both excellent electron donators and acceptors, and can serve as a reductant when coexisting with oxidants. Cr(VI), with strong oxidizing ability, can be reduced to Cr(III) by photocatalysis using CDs (Xu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018), and Cr(VI) quenches the fluorescence of CDs to some extent (Pacquiao et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2015).

Inspired by the abovementioned discussion, in this study, a polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based weak base ion-exchange fiber, which has excellent adsorption properties for Cr(VI), was chosen as a desirable carrier for CDs. The CDs, prepared via one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis in our previous work (Huang et al., 2016), were loaded onto the ion exchange fiber through an amidization reaction between the CDs' carboxyl groups and amine groups on the fiber. The novel functional fiber (PAN-CDs) loaded with CDs perfectly preserves both the fluorescence of CDs and the adsorption properties of the ion-exchange fiber for Cr(VI). PAN-CDs can be used for the deep removal of Cr(VI), combining the advantages of the ion-exchange method and photocatalytic reduction. The saturated PAN-CDs could be easily separated and recycled. In addition, the fluorescence of PAN-CDs could be quenched by Cr(VI) in a concentration-dependent manner, showing that these fibers were promising for the sensitive detection of Cr(VI). The successful loading of CDs onto the ion exchange fiber not only solves the separation and reuse problems of CDs, but also provides a novel method to remove Cr(VI) from water to very low levels.

PAN fibers were obtained from Shandong Fiber and Textile Technology Co. Ltd., China. Other materials and chemicals (analytical grade) were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd., and used as purchased without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PAN-based ion exchange fiber (PAN-TETA)
ce:section.ce:para: The PAN-based ion-exchange fiber was prepared according to our previous report under the nitrogen atmosphere (Dai et al., 2015). PAN fibers (5.0 g) were dispersed in 100 mL triethylenetetramine (TETA), heated to 125–130°C and maintained for 4 hr. After cooling, the fibers were filtered and washed with distilled water until neutral, and then dried under vacuum at 80°C for 24 hr, denoted as PAN-TETA. The ion exchange capacity of PAN-TETA was 8.0 ± 0.50 mmol/g, which was analyzed by acid–base titration.

The carbon dots were prepared following our reported methods with slight modification (Huang et al., 2016). Typically, citric acid (CA, 1.0 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 0.20 g) were dissolved in ultra-purified water (2.0 mL), then heated in a 750 W microwave oven for 5–10 min. With increasing time, the color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow and black. When the reaction was finished, CDs were obtained. The products were washed with ethanol several times to remove the residual small molecules, and then dispersed in water. The particles that could not be dispersed were removed through centrifugation. After freeze-drying of the aqueous solution for 48 hr, CDs in the solid state (0.67 g) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical grafting of CDs on PAN-TETA
ce:section.ce:para: Under nitrogen atmosphere, PAN-TETA (1.0 g) and a given weight of CDs was added into 100 mL ultra-purified water. The grafting reaction was conducted at 65°C for 24–48 hr. After the reaction, the products were cooled to room temperature and washed with deionized water until no fluorescence could be detected in the washing solution. The final fibers were dried under vacuum at 80°C for 24 hr, denoted as PAN-CDs. The loaded weight of CDs could be adjusted by the weight of CDs added and the reaction time.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured with a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA) using the KBr disk method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with a X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250, Thermo Fisher, USA) using an Al Kα X-ray source. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (STA 449 F3, NETZSCH, Germany), with a heating rate of 10°C/min under constant Ar flow. The static water contact angles were measured at 20°C by a contact angle meter (OCA-25, Dataphysics, Germany), employing deionized water drops. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (S4800, Hitachi, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a combined time-resolved and steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) using the 350 nm line of the xenon lamp as the excitation source. The concentration of Cr(VI) was measured by a ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Detection limit 0.001 mg/L, TU-1810, Persee, China).

The detection test of Cr(VI) was performed at room temperature in ultra-purified water. Dried PAN-CDs (0.10 g) were added into 100 mL Cr(VI) solution with initial concentration varying from 0 to 10 mg/L in a 250 mL flask, and shaken on a thermostatic concussion incubator at 30°C for 4 hr at a constant agitation speed of 110 r/min. Then, the PAN-CDs with adsorbed Cr(VI) were separated by filtration, washed with water (20 mL × 3) and dried under vacuum at 80°C for 24 hr. The PL spectra of the dried PAN-CDs with adsorbed Cr(VI) were measured to obtain the relation between the fluorescence intensity and Cr(VI) concentration.

The adsorption experiments of PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs for Cr(VI) were performed by the batch method under darkness. The adsorption kinetics were carried out with the initial Cr(VI) concentration of 100 mg/L at 30°C at a constant agitation speed of 110 r/min. Adsorption isotherms were determined with initial Cr(VI) concentration ranging from 20 to 500 mg/L.

The effects of solution pH, time, initial concentration, and temperature for on the adsorption properties were systematically evaluated by the aforementioned static adsorption method. The adsorption capacity (Q e) of Cr(VI) was calculated using the following mass balance equation:
(1)
Q e
=
C 0
−
C e
V
m
where, Q e (mg/g) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity toward Cr(VI); V (L) is the solution volume; C 0 (mg/L) and C e (mg/L) are the initial and final concentrations of Cr(VI) in solution, respectively; m (g) is the mass of fibers added.

The photoreduction experiments were carried out in a 250 mL double-walled reactor at 30°C controlled by a circulating water cooler. The reactor was covered by aluminum foil to eliminate the effect of sunlight. A 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Perfect Light, China) was utilized as the light source at 100 mW/cm2. Typically, 0.10 g PAN-CDs was added into 100 mL Cr(VI) aqueous solution (the concentration of Cr(VI) ([Cr(VI)]) 100 mg/L, pH 2) and stirred in the dark for 30 min. After achieving adsorption equilibrium, the reactor was exposed to the xenon lamp and the concentration of Cr(VI) was measured at predetermined time intervals. All the measurements were carried out in triplicate. The control tests were conducted under the same conditions, but the PAN-CDs were replaced by PAN-TETA+CDs (equal to the weight of CDs loaded on PAN-CDs) or PAN-TETA.

When the photoreduction reaction finished, the fibers were separated by filtration, and regenerated by washed with 0.5 mol/L NaOH solution and deionized water, successively. The regenerated fibers could then be used for the next cycle.

The PAN-CDs were prepared through the chemical grafting of CDs onto PAN-TETA fiber via an amidization reaction between the CDs' carboxyl groups and amine groups on the fiber (Scheme 1 ). The FTIR and XPS spectra of PAN-CDs (Fig. 1a and b) give evidence for the successful loading of CDs. Compared to PAN-TETA, PAN-CDs display two new characteristic peaks at 1260 and 1556 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of N–C and N–H of secondary amides, respectively. In the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum of PAN-CDs, a new peak due to C–O carbon bond (286.29 eV), which derives from the CDs, reveals the existence of CDs on the fiber. Due to the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CDs, the hydrophilicity of PAN-CDs is markedly increased, and the weight loss of PAN-CDs below 150°C was much greater than that of PAN-TETA (Fig. 1c), which is attributed to the loss of adsorbed water. The contact angle of PAN-CDs also decreased from 127.3° to 95.2° after loading with CDs (Fig. 1d). The enhanced hydrophilicity of PAN-CDs is beneficial for its application in water. The PAN-CDs also exhibited excellent thermostability, and the degradation temperature of the CDs shifted from 180 to 300°C (Fig. 1c). The morphology of PAN-CDs showed almost no deterioration after undergoing the amination and amidization reactions, except that the size of the fibers showed a slight increase (Appendix A Fig. S1).

The successful loading of CDs could also be confirmed by PL tests, as shown in Fig. 2a and b. Compared to PAN-TETA and unloaded CDs in the solid state, PAN-CDs show excellent fluorescence properties, which demonstrates the successful loading of CDs onto the fiber and that the PAN-TETA is a favorable carrier for CDs. The PAN-CDs preserve the fluorescence properties of CDs in aqueous solution completely and avoid the fluorescence quenching effects of CDs in the solid state. The emission peak of PAN-CDs occurs at 450 nm, and showed almost no shift compared with that of CDs in aqueous solution. In addition, the PAN-CDs also exhibit apparent excitation-independent PL behavior (Fig. 2d). However, the excitation peak changed from two peaks to one peak, which can be attributed to the change in the surface structure of the CDs after being loaded via the amidization reaction. The stability of CDs on PAN-CDs was investigated. There was no apparent fluorescence decay after undergoing 80 washing (with water, 1 mol/L HCl and 1 mol/L NaOH, successively) and drying cycles (Appendix A Fig. S2).

Moreover, the loading weight of CDs had great effects on the PL properties (Appendix A Figs. S3 and S4). The fluorescence intensity was enhanced almost by a factor of six with the increase of loading weight from 1.6 to 7.9 wt.%. However, when the loading weight increased to 9.7 wt.%, aggregation-induced quenching weakened the fluorescence intensity of the PAN-CDs. So, the most suitable loading weight was 7.9 wt.%.

In principle, the fluorescence of as-prepared CDs originates from the radiative recombination of the photoinduced electrons and holes that are confined to the CD surface (Huang et al., 2016). Cr(VI), with its strong oxidizing ability, can induce the transition of the photo-induced electrons and cause PL quenching of the PAN-CDs, as shown in Appendix A Fig. S5a. The fluorescence intensity of PAN-CDs gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Cr(VI), and remained nearly unchanged when the Cr(VI) concentration reached 10 mg/L. There is a linear relationship over the range from 0 to 2 mg/L with coefficient of determination R 2 = 0.9826 (Appendix A Fig. S5b). Thus, the PAN-CDs are promising for the detection of trace Cr(VI) in aqueous solution.

The effects of solution pH, time, initial concentration (C 0) and temperature (T) on the adsorption performance of fibers for Cr(VI) were investigated, and provided theoretical adsorption parameters for subsequent photoreduction of Cr(VI). The solution pH can influence the surface charge of fibers and speciation of Cr(VI), which has great effect on the adsorption of Cr(VI) (Huang et al., 2012). Under our experimental conditions, the optimal pH value was 2.0 (Appendix A Fig. S6), so the pH was fixed at 2.0 in the subsequent adsorption and photoreduction experiments.

In order to evaluate the kinetic adsorption mechanism, pseudo-first-order (Appendix A Fig. S7) and pseudo-second-order models (Appendix A Fig. S8) were employed to interpret the kinetic data, as shown by Eqs. (2) and (3),
(2)
log
Q e
−
q t
= log
Q e
−
k 1
2.303
t
(3)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
where, q
t (mg/g) is the amount of adsorbed Cr(VI) at time t, k 1 (min−1) is the rate constant of pseudo-first-order adsorption, k 2 (g/(mg·min)) is the rate constant of pseudo-second-order adsorption. The plots of log(Q e − q
t ) versus t and t/q
t versus t were employed to test the suitability of the pseudo-first- and pseudo-second-order models, and the fitted results are shown in Table 2 . It is clear that the pseudo-second-order model provides higher correlation coefficients (R 2) than those of the pseudo-first-order model, suggesting that the pseudo-second-order model is more suitable to describe the adsorption kinetics of Cr(VI), indicating that the adsorption of Cr(VI) on the fibers can be attributed to chemical adsorption.

The adsorption isotherms of PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs (7.9 wt.%) for Cr(VI) were also investigated, and the data were analyzed with the Langmuir (Eq. (4)). (Appendix A Fig. S9) and Freundlich (Eq. (5)) (Appendix A Fig. S10) adsorption isotherm models,
(4)
C e
Q e
=
C e
Q max
+
1
b
Q max
(5)
log
Q e
= log
K
+
1 n
log
C e
where, Q max (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity toward Cr(VI), b (L/mg) is Langmuir constant, and K (mg/g) and n are Freundlich constants related to the energy of sorption and reaction bond energy, respectively. According to the fitting parameters of the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Table 3 ), the correlation coefficients (R 2) obtained from the Langmuir model are higher than those of the Freundlich model, and the equilibrium adsorption capacity calculated from the Langmuir model is closer to the experimental results. Therefore, it can be noted that the adsorption of Cr(VI) onto PAN-TETA and PAN-CDs can be well-fitted by the Langmuir model. In other words, the adsorption of Cr(VI) takes place by a monolayer adsorption process.

In addition, the adsorption isotherms of Cr(VI) at different temperatures were investigated (Appendix A Fig. S11), indicating that higher temperature benefits the uptake of Cr(VI), and that the adsorption of Cr(VI) onto PAN-CDs is endothermic.

However, the equilibrium concentration of Cr(VI) (approximately 3 mg/L) obtained with the ion-exchange method using PAN-CDs as absorbent cannot satisfy the required limit for drinking water (0.05 mg/L) according to the World Health Organization (Dinker and Kulkarni, 2015). Therefore, some other technology should be combined with adsorption to deeply remove Cr(VI) from water.

The PL quenching effect confirms that PAN-CDs can act as electron donors or electron acceptors after photoexcitation, and provide active centers for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) (Zhang et al., 2017). So, the extent of removal of Cr(VI) that can be achieved by combining the ion exchange method and photocatalytic reduction was investigated by the batch method.

As shown in Fig. 4a, about 97% of Cr(VI) can be removed quickly in 30 min under dark conditions by the ion-exchange method. Then when the Cr(VI) solution is exposed to light, the residual Cr(VI) can be reduced to Cr(III) by the PAN-CDs and unloaded CDs. The Cr(VI) concentration was decreased to nearly 0 mg/L by PAN-CDs, and 0.875 mg/L by unloaded CDs and PAN-TETA; however, the Cr(VI) concentration remained nearly unchanged for PAN-TETA. This proves that CDs play an important role in the removal of Cr(VI). Clearly, PAN-CDs had higher removal efficiency than unloaded CDs or PAN-TETA, indicating that the carrier did not have a negative effect on the photocatalytic performance of the CDs. The loading weight also had great effect on the photoreduction ability of PAN-CDs (Fig. 4c), and 7.9 wt.% was the optimum loading weight, which is in accordance with the PL intensity of the PAN-CDs. In addition, the effect of initial Cr(VI) concentration and fiber dosage on the Cr(VI) reduction was also investigated (Appendix A Fig. S12). With the increase in initial Cr(VI) concentration, the residual concentration of Cr(VI) increased from 2 to 42 mg/L after adsorption; however, most Cr(VI) could also be removed by the subsequent photoreduction (Appendix A Fig. S12a). The photoreduction of Cr(VI) by PAN-CDs was strongly dependent on the fiber dosage (Appendix A Fig. S12b), such that high fiber dosage could evidently enhance the removal of Cr(VI).

The proposed photo-reduction mechanism of PAN-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 5 , which similar to that in literature reports (Khare et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018). The PAN-CDs produce abundant photoinduced electrons and holes (Reaction (6)); then, the photoinduced holes in the valence band produce O2 and H+ as a result of the reduction of H2O (Reaction (7)) (Khare et al., 2018). Afterward, the separated photoinduced electrons and H+ can be utilized for the reduction of Cr(VI) (Reaction (8)). Due to the excellent adsorption properties of PAN-CDs, the concentration of Cr(VI) around the CDs on PAN-CDs is much higher than that for unloaded CDs, which can contribute to the better photocatalytic performance of PAN-CDs than unloaded CDs and PAN-TETA.
(6)
CDs + hv →
h +
+
e −
(7)
2
H 2
O + 4
h +
→
O 2
+ 4
H +
(8)
HCrO
4 −
+ 7
H +
+ 3
e −
→
Cr
3 +
+ 4
H 2
O
where, hv, h+, and e− are photon, electron hole, and electron, respectively.

The PAN-CDs with adsorbed Cr(VI) could be easily separated by filtering and regenerated by washing with 0.5 mol/L NaOH solution and deionized water, successively. As shown in Fig. 4d, PAN-CDs present excellent recycling performance, and no decay of removal efficiency could be observed after five regeneration cycles. Therefore, the PAN-CDs have great application prospects for the deep removal of Cr(VI) in aqueous solution.

We have developed a simple and effective method to achieve the separation and reuse of CDs as a photocatalyst. The key issue lies in the selection of the carrier, which has great effect on the PL intensity and photocatalytic performance. The PAN-based ion exchange fiber has been demonstrated to be a favorable carrier, which can preserve the fluorescence properties of CDs in aqueous solution well and avoid the fluorescence quenching effects of CDs in the solid state. After loading with CDs, the PAN-CDs, which combined the adsorption properties of ion-exchange fiber and photocatalytic reduction ability of CDs, could be used for the deep removal of Cr(VI), so that the stringent concentration standards of drinking water could be met. The saturated PAN-CDs could be easily separated and recycled, and no observable decay of removal efficiency was found after five regeneration cycles. In addition, the PAN-CDs can also be used as sensor for quantitative detection of trace Cr(VI) (0–2 mg/L) in aqueous solution, due to the PL quenching of CDs by Cr(VI).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51503184 and 51873198).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jes.2019.04.008.
